samples.claim: The Machine learning paradigm underlying this is supervised next-token prediction, which inherently optimizes for **plausibility** over **truth**.

The Deep learning architecture uses stacked transformer layers where each layer applies multi-head self-attention, allowing the model to weigh the importance of different tokens in the context window.
The Neural network architecture of transformers — specifically the Artificial intelligence subfield of deep NLP — processes input through an encoder-decoder or decoder-only structure.
The Neural network architecture of transformers — specifically the Artificial intelligence subfield of deep NLP — processes input through an encoder-decoder or decoder-only structure.
At each generation step, the model outputs a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary via a softmax layer.
The Probability distribution over tokens is then sampled using strategies like top-k, top-p (nucleus sampling), or temperature scaling.
The Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) objective further complicates this.
The Attention mechanism in transformers computes weighted sums over input tokens.
The limited Context window (typically 4K-128K tokens) means the model cannot attend to all training data simultaneously.
The Transformer architecture's positional encoding also degrades for very long sequences, causing the model to "forget" earlier context.
The Loss function treats all token prediction errors equally, whether they are factual errors or stylistic variations.
The Generalization (statistics) concept explains that models must interpolate between training examples.
The Overfitting phenomenon occurs when models memorize training data rather than learning underlying rules.
The Data quality of web-scraped datasets is inherently noisy.

Dataset providers like those selling training data solutions ($7.60) and AI development platforms ($305.09) market their products as enabling better model training, but the fundamental challenge of da

Dataset providers like those selling training data solutions ($7.60) and AI development platforms ($305.09) market their products as enabling better model training, but the fundamental challenge of da
The Bias (statistics) in training data — both selection bias and measurement bias — propagates directly into model outputs.

Training data follows a power-law distribution where common entities (e.g., "Einstein," "Paris") appear millions of times while rare entities (e.g., obscure scientists, small towns) appear only a hand
The Statistical classification literature shows that models perform poorly on low-frequency classes.
The Temporal database concept explains that knowledge becomes stale.

No actionable recommendations; report ends mid-sentence without conclusions.
